topic: Antarctic gravity hole — H(r)→g coupling and WGS84-derived Domain 4 formula
change_summary: Decider self-applied patch (commit db60a9c, ISS-1329 closed) rewrote `finding`/`tldr_evidence`/`tldr_verdict` to lead with a 1,430× error figure, replacing the prior 170× headline (finding 88→167 chars). The patch did NOT propagate to `detail_evidence` or `detail_verdict_text`, which still defend 170× as the primary ratio.
our_argument_summary: Dome's H(r) function predicts ~90% gravity loss at the Antarctic rim under any monotonic H→g coupling; observed Antarctic gravity variation is ~0.5%. The only working dome gravity formula (Domain 4 g(r) = 9.7803×(1+0.005307×exp(-r/λg))) is the WGS84 normal gravity formula in dome notation, and even this WGS84-clone violates Domain 4's own ≤0.1% falsification threshold when evaluated at the rim (4.8× over). 'Sub-terrestrial structure' is a label, not a mechanism. Verdict: Self-Contradicted.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: Domain 4 in 08-live-power.txt: Formula g(r) = 9.7803×(1+0.005307×exp(-r/λg)); evaluated at r=0 only, where exp(0)=1 collapses to g = 9.7803×(1+0.005307) = 9.8322 m/s² — the WGS84 polar gravity. Domain 19 ('Mascon Gravity'): one-sided threshold 'Anomaly <20 mGal' with no model prediction. monitor.py contains no gravity-anomaly fetch for Antarctic mascons.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Hardcoded identity: 9.7803 (WGS84 equatorial g) and 0.005307 (WGS84 gravity flattening) reproduce 9.8322 (WGS84 polar g) by construction. No dome-specific physics enters.
data_sources_used: WGS84 reference values (no external fetch in monitor.py for this WIN)
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: Domain 19 uses a one-sided threshold ('Anomaly <20 mGal') against the real -40 to -60 mGal Bouguer anomaly, so any honest measurement trivially exceeds the threshold and counts as 'PASS' — pass-by-threshold, not pass-by-prediction.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None — the review accurately identifies the WGS84 derivation chain and the Domain 19 inverted falsification logic.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: Domain 4 (08-live-power.txt line 70) prints 'Predicted: 9.832204 | Observed: 9.8322 | Status: PASS' (line 221). The 'predicted' value is computed from the WGS84 polar value with a formula whose exponential term is identically 1 at r=0. No gravity API/fetch is invoked for this WIN — it is a static identity check.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Antarctic gravity low is fully explained by standard geophysics (mantle plume beneath East Antarctic Plateau; incomplete post-glacial isostatic adjustment; dynamic topography from mantle convection — Pappa et al. 2019, Ferraccioli et al. 2011). The dome relabels this as 'Sub-terrestrial structure' (Domain 19) without an equation. The Domain 4 gravity formula is the WGS84 normal-gravity formula in different notation.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: GRACE/GRACE-FO Level-2 gravity products (continuous since 2002) and Bouguer-anomaly compilations (Pappa 2019; Ferraccioli 2011; Forte 2010) were public well before any dome monitor commit. The monitor.py target value is hardcoded.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No dome-specific equation links 'Sub-terrestrial structure' to a numerical gravity prediction at any (r, lat). The Domain 4 formula uses WGS84 constants (9.7803, 0.005307) directly. The H(r)-implied 90% drop is what the dome's own geometry would predict if H(r) couples to g monotonically — the dome avoids stating any such coupling explicitly, which leaves the mechanism vacuous.

holes_found.description: INTERNAL INCONSISTENCY between surface text (1,430×) and detail body (170×). The patch applied at commit db60a9c (ISS-1329 closed) updated `finding`, `tldr_evidence`, and `tldr_verdict` to lead with the 1,430× headline, but `detail_evidence` and `detail_verdict_text` still defend 170× as the primary number. detail_verdict_text reads 'actual variation is 0.53% (ratio: 90%/0.53% ≈ 170×)' verbatim. A defender can truthfully say: your own review cannot decide whether the error is 170× or 1,430× — which is it? The 1,430× number is only derived in detail_extra paragraph (ii); detail_evidence never mentions it.
holes_found.recommendation: Propagate the 1,430× framing into detail_evidence and detail_verdict_text. Specifically: detail_evidence's closing paragraph 'the dome's predicted variation exceeds the observed variation by 90%/0.53% ≈ 170×' should be rewritten to lead with the apples-to-apples 1,430× ratio (predicted drop ÷ observed drop, both in m/s²) and demote 170× to a parenthetical conservative lower bound. detail_verdict_text's parenthetical should match. Both numbers are already correct mathematically; the patch was simply incomplete.

holes_found.description: GEOGRAPHIC MISLABEL of '~9.826 m/s² Antarctic-interior gravity'. The detail_extra paragraph (ii) characterises 9.826 m/s² as 'observed Antarctic-interior gravity' but 9.826 m/s² is the WGS84 normal gravity at ~70°S — the Antarctic coast/Southern Ocean transition, NOT the interior plateau. Interior values (80–90°S) are 9.8306–9.8322 m/s². If a defender looks up gravity for interior Antarctica and finds 9.830, the review appears to have inflated the 'Antarctic interior' baseline. The irony is that this makes 1,430× CONSERVATIVE — using true 80°S gravity (9.8306) gives a ratio of 5,593× (verified). But the labeling is loose enough to be pickable.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) drop the word 'interior' and write 'Antarctic-latitude gravity (the value cited in the objection, ~9.826 m/s², corresponding to ~70°S)' — making explicit that this is a geographically-conservative choice that anchors the comparison to data the defender themselves would accept; or (b) re-anchor to true interior (9.8306 at 80°S) and let the ratio float upward to ~5,600×. Option (a) is rhetorically safer because it preempts the cherry-pick attack; option (b) is mathematically more honest. The current text is the worst of both — claims 'interior' but uses a coastal value.

holes_found.description: FINDING DOES NOT DISCLOSE LINEAR-COUPLING ASSUMPTION. The 1,430× ratio is computed under linear g ∝ H(r) coupling (g_pred_rim = g_polar × H_rim/H_pole). The detail_evidence body acknowledges multiple scalings (linear → 90% drop, square-root → 69%, H^(1/5) → 38%) but the new finding text says 'apples-to-apples measurement yields 1,430× error' without disclosing which coupling produces 1,430×. Under H^(1/5) (the most forgiving scaling discussed) the apples-to-apples ratio drops to ~589× (verified: g_pred_rim_pow = 9.8322 × (837/8537)^0.2 = 6.179 m/s², drop = 3.65 m/s², 3.65/0.0062 ≈ 589). A defender can say: you are attacking the most aggressive scaling I never committed to.
holes_found.recommendation: Edit finding to read 'Dome's own H(r) implies ≥38% gravity drop at Antarctic rim under any monotonic coupling (1,430× error under linear coupling; ~590× under the weakest physically-plausible H^(1/5) coupling).' This preserves the headline number while disclosing the assumption and proving the critique survives the most defender-friendly choice.

holes_found.description: MISSING DOME CITATION FOR 'gravity depends on H(r)' ASSUMPTION. The detail_evidence asserts 'the dome model's stated physics links gravity to the aetheric pressure gradient driven by column height H(r)' but provides no raw-text citation. The dome's only quantitative gravity formula (Domain 4) does NOT use H(r) at all — it uses a WGS84-derived exponential. The aetheric-pressure narrative does mention 'aetheric pressure gradient drives both [SAA] and [Roaring 40s]' (raw-text/02-wins.txt line 31) but never explicitly says g = f(H(r)). A careful defender can deny the implication: 'I never said gravity depends on column height; I only said aether pressure drives geomagnetic and atmospheric anomalies.' The critique then collapses to 'the dome has no quantitative gravity mechanism beyond a WGS84 reskin' — which is still a strong critique, but a different one.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a citation or acknowledge the gap. Either (a) cite the specific dome line that ties gravity to H(r) if it exists (search 02-wins.txt, 05-model.txt, 08-live-power.txt for any explicit g↔H(r) statement); or (b) reframe the H(r)-implication as 'IF aether pressure depends on column height H(r) AND gravity depends on aether pressure (both stated in dome narrative), THEN gravity is a monotonic function of H(r) — but the dome refuses to commit to a specific coupling, which is itself the problem.' Option (b) is more defensible and tightens the existing detail_evidence.

holes_found.description: Shepherd et al. 2012 (DOI 10.1126/science.1228102) is cited for 'incomplete post-glacial isostatic adjustment from the Last Glacial Maximum ice sheet, measured directly by GRACE satellite gravimetry'. That paper is 'A Reconciled Estimate of Ice-Sheet Mass Balance' — it covers GRACE-derived ice mass balance, not GIA per se. Stronger citations for GIA contributions to Antarctic gravity would be Whitehouse et al. 2012 (ICE-6G_C) or Ivins et al. 2013 (IJ05_R2 GIA model).
holes_found.recommendation: Replace the Shepherd 2012 citation with Whitehouse et al. 2012 (Quaternary Science Reviews, doi:10.1016/j.quascirev.2011.11.016) or Ivins et al. 2013 (JGR Solid Earth, doi:10.1002/jgrb.50208). Both are direct GIA-modelling papers used by GRACE/GRACE-FO for Antarctic mass balance corrections.

stronger_arguments.description: The 'Mascon Gravity ↔ SAA Boundary: r=0.92' correlation in Domain 19 is itself a known geophysical correlation explained by the same mantle plume that drives the Antarctic gravity low — the East Antarctic mantle plume is geographically near the SAA's southern fringe. The dome correctly observes a real spatial coincidence, but standard geodynamics (mantle convection, core-mantle boundary heat flux) explains BOTH features as products of the same deep-mantle structure. The dome reads a single shared cause as evidence of a shared mechanism ('Sub-terrestrial structure'); standard physics also reads it as a single shared cause (mantle dynamics) but identifies the mechanism. This is a textbook 'standard model already explains the correlation' situation.
sources: Olson 2016 'Mantle Control of the Geodynamo: Consequences of Top-Down Regulation', Reviews of Geophysics
Heyn, Conrad, Trønnes 2018 'Stabilizing Effect of Compositional Viscosity Contrasts on Thermochemical Piles', Geophysical Research Letters

stronger_arguments.description: The Domain 4 falsification threshold ('Model error >0.1% vs WGS84 standard') is a self-imposed binding constraint that the dome's only gravity formula violates at r=20,015 km (verified: g_D4_rim = 9.7854 m/s², error = 0.476% > 0.1% by a factor of 4.8×). This means the dome's own published falsification logic, applied to its own published formula at its own rim radius, classifies the model as FAILED. The author has not amended either the threshold or the formula. This is a stronger framing than the H(r) critique because it requires no external assumption about coupling — the dome falsifies itself by its own published rule.

kernel_of_truth.description: The Antarctic gravity low IS a real, well-characterised geophysical feature. The spatial correlation between the gravity anomaly and the SAA boundary (r=0.92 per the dome's own Domain 19) IS genuinely interesting and reflects a shared deep-mantle origin. The dome author correctly identifies that something localized in the Antarctic crust/mantle is producing both gravitational and geomagnetic signatures. The Domain 4 formula, evaluated at r=0 (the equatorial reference point), DOES reproduce WGS84 polar gravity to better than 0.001% — that is a real numerical match.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Identifying a real correlation does not constitute a prediction; the dome adopts the published value and renames the cause. The Domain 4 numerical match at r=0 is tautological (the formula was constructed from WGS84 constants to reproduce WGS84 outputs). Renaming 'mantle plume + GIA' as 'Sub-terrestrial structure' provides no new empirical content. A model that scores PASS on all its own checks while predicting a 90% gravity drop that doesn't occur is self-falsifying.

advocate_mode.best_defense: A fair-minded dome defender (one who has accepted the review's six methodological principles) would attack the inconsistency itself. The defense: 'Your headline says 1,430× and your detail body says 170×. Pick one and defend it. The 1,430× number is computed under a linear H→g coupling I never published; the 170× number divides two dimensionless variation ratios and is structurally unsound — you cannot divide a 90% prediction by a 0.53% measurement and call the quotient an error of equivalent type. The 170× ratio compares the predicted CHANGE in g to the observed CHANGE in g, expressed as percentages of different baselines (predicted is fractional drop from polar to rim; observed is fractional spread across the entire globe). The 1,430× ratio compares predicted drop in m/s² to observed drop in m/s² — that is a cleaner quantity, but it presupposes my linear coupling. Until you produce a dome statement committing me to either H→g linear coupling OR a specific geographic identification of rim with Antarctic interior, your critique attacks straw positions of two different shapes simultaneously.' This defense is not trivially refuted because the review IS internally inconsistent on this exact point.
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Resolve the inconsistency in this cycle and add an explicit definitions sentence: 'The 1,430× ratio = (dome-predicted gravity drop from pole to rim under linear H(r)→g coupling) ÷ (observed gravity drop from pole to Antarctic-latitude where r=20,015 maps geographically). Both quantities are in m/s². The conservative 170× ratio = (dome-predicted fractional gravity variation, ~90%) ÷ (observed fractional variation across the globe, ~0.53%). Either reading the model fails by orders of magnitude. The dome has not published a non-linear coupling that would reduce these ratios — under H^(1/5), the most-forgiving monotonic alternative, the apples-to-apples ratio is still ~590×.' Then patch detail_evidence and detail_verdict_text to use the same framing.

cross_win_consistency.notes: WIN-012 (eclipse gravity coupling κ=1.67) attacks dome gravity from the time-varying side (eclipse anomaly basis); WIN-067 attacks from the spatial side (latitude variation). Both reach 'Self-Contradicted' on independent grounds. WIN-044 (FSF from V12 geometry) shares the H(r) function and is also Self-Contradicted; the two arguments reinforce each other (H(r) is bad for gravity AND bad for distance scaling). WIN-027 (Southern distance quadratic) uses the H(r) machinery for distance, again reinforcing rather than contradicting. WIN-066 (SAA-related) shares the dome's claim about Antarctic regional anomalies; both are downgraded by the standard-physics mantle-plume explanation. No cross-WIN contradictions found.

claims_checked: Domain 4 g(rim) = 9.7854 m/s², error 0.476% vs WGS84 polar (violates 0.1% threshold by 4.8×)
quantitative_verification.computations: Verified by python3: H(20015) = 8537·exp(-20015/8619) = 837.12 km (matches review's 837); g_pred_rim = 9.8322 × 837/8537 = 0.964 m/s² (matches); 9.8322 - 0.964 = 8.868 m/s² (matches); 170× check: 90/0.53 = 169.8 (matches); 1,430× check: 8.868/0.0062 = 1,430.3 (matches); Domain 4 at r=20015: 9.7803·(1 + 0.005307·exp(-20015/8619)) = 9.7854 m/s², |9.7854 - 9.8322|/9.8322 = 0.476% (matches review claim that this violates Domain 4's own 0.1% threshold). 9.826 m/s² corresponds to latitude ~70°S using the Somigliana formula (g_a=9.7803253359, g_b=9.8321849378, WGS84 ellipsoid) — NOT 'interior' Antarctica (80°S = 9.8306, 90°S = 9.8322). H^(1/5) coupling: g_pred_rim_pow = 9.8322·(837/8537)^0.2 = 6.179 m/s², drop = 3.653 m/s², ratio 3.653/0.0062 ≈ 589× — confirms detail_evidence's claim that even 'extreme g ∝ H^(1/5) coupling still gives a 38% drop' and lets us put a specific 589× number on the most-forgiving apples-to-apples ratio.
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: The '~9.826 m/s² Antarctic-interior gravity' label is geographically incorrect: 9.826 corresponds to ~70°S (Antarctic coast), not interior (80–90°S, where g ≥ 9.830). The 1,430× ratio is therefore CONSERVATIVE (true interior gives ~5,600×), but the label should be corrected. All other arithmetic checks out exactly.

deeper_analysis:
WIN-067 has matured through five prior cycles and is one of the more robust Self-Contradicted entries. The most recent edit (commit db60a9c, 22 Apr 02:36 UTC) closed ISS-1329 by promoting the 1,430× apples-to-apples ratio to the surface text — a defensible move because 1,430× is a cleaner drop ÷ drop quantity than 170× (% ÷ %, which mixes baselines). The patch is INCOMPLETE: the detailed body still defends 170× as primary, creating a textual inconsistency that a careful defender would exploit. The fix is mechanical (propagate the new framing into detail_evidence and detail_verdict_text) and should be the highest-priority follow-up.

On the substantive critique: the Domain 4 self-falsification finding is the strongest argument here. The dome publishes both a formula (g(r) = 9.7803·(1+0.005307·exp(-r/λg))) and a falsification rule (error >0.1% vs WGS84). At the rim, the formula gives 9.7854 m/s², which is 0.476% off WGS84 — failing by 4.8× the threshold. This is the dome convicting itself on the dome's own scoreboard. It requires no assumption about H→g coupling. The H(r)-implication argument is weaker because it presupposes a coupling the dome refuses to commit to, but it is still useful as the wider envelope (any monotonic coupling gives ≥38% drop, 1,430× under linear, ~590× under H^(1/5)).

The verdict 'Self-Contradicted' continues to hold under both arguments. Recommended action: minor_edit to fix the 170×/1,430× inconsistency and tighten the geographic labeling around '9.826 m/s² Antarctic-interior'.

citations_unchecked: Pappa et al. 2019, JGR, DOI 10.1029/2019JB017997 — DOI structure plausible but paper title/contents not independently fetched this run

Shepherd et al. 2012, Science, DOI 10.1126/science.1228102 — paper title is 'A Reconciled Estimate of Ice-Sheet Mass Balance', which is about GRACE-derived mass balance, not GIA per se. Citation is loose (see hole #5)
Ferraccioli et al. 2011, Nature, DOI 10.1038/nature09659 — DOI structure plausible; paper relevance to East Antarctic Bouguer anomaly is correct as far as we know
